subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Legal Challenges vs. Social Movements for Civil Rights Policy Change
stimulus: In the United States, groups use different methods in order to achieve their policy goals. Take a position on whether legal challenges or social movements are the most effective means for changing federal policy in the area of civil rights.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: The Federalist 78, The First Amendment, "Letter from Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Legal challenges are most effective because decisions of the court do not necessarily need to appeal to public opinion and have the effect of changing policy in the area of civil rights.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “Social movements are most effective because large numbers of people can put pressure on the political system which has resulted in policy changes in the area of civil rights.”

wrong_examples: “History has shown that legal challenges, which use the courts are clearly the most effective in changing federal policy when it comes to civil rights.” Do not respond to the prompt
“Social movements work outside of the system and can mobilize citizens.” Responses that earn this point:
Respond to the prompt rather than restating or rephrasing the prompt and establish a line of reasoning.
Provide a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning about whether legal challenges or social movements are the most effective means for changing federal policy.
common_traps: Do NOT require foundational documents in thesis
A because clause with reasons IS a line of reasoning

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award HIGHEST tier met. Each tier 1pt progressive. Tier 2+ requires Row A.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: One relevant piece of evidence.
Evidence Tier 2: One specific piece supporting thesis OR two relevant. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece supporting thesis OR two relevant
alternatives.correct_example: Uses evidence to directly support thesis.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Two specific pieces supporting thesis, one from listed foundational document. Requires Row A.

alternatives.correct_example: Uses foundational doc + concept supporting thesis.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Simply stating that evidence supports the claim without explaining the connection does not earn this point.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Court cases such as Brown v. Board of Education established a precedent for desegregating schools which was one of the biggest achievements of the Civil Rights movement. Because schools are locally controlled, it would have been much more difficult to achieve such a broad victory through a social movement.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “Political protest and civil disobedience are actions that can be taken when direct challenges to the system have been exhausted or are no longer effective. When a social movement gains prominent recognition, legislatures are more likely to move quickly to address the concerns of their citizens, unlike a legal challenge which can be costly and slow.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut — not merely acknowledge it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some argue that social movements are better because they mobilize a large number of people for change, but social movements require coordination and participation which takes time.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “Some argue that legal challenges are more effective because they don’t require changing public opinion, but legal challenges generally require financial support and could take a long time.”

wrong_examples: “Many would argue that legal challenges are better, but this is simply untrue.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not refute, concede, or rebut that perspective
“Some would argue that social movements are better because they mobilize people for change.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
